comment.body: Bit of a telltale last night was the size of the after hours trade... i generally look at the 30min after close volume....if you look at last two weeks for SPY you can see Fri 28 9mill, mon 3.5mill,Tue 5.2mill,Wed 3.8mill,Thurs 4.3mill, Fri 3.5mill, Mon 5.0 mill, Tue 4.3mill, wed 4.06 mill, Thurs 9 mill. If you the analyse what happened next two days and measure SPY close versus lowest point you will see that on Friday 28th we had 20 point drop from 592 to 572. The rest of the days when we had much lower after hr volume you had much smaller moves...last night we had another 9 mill so lets see over next two days. It is not the whole story because on days with big move you often see large number of trades reported late the next day. These trades are conducted on the London exchange i believe which means they can be hidden for extra time from markets. Also there could be other reasons for large volume like opex etc...but away from special dates its not a bad rule of thumb...I think these after hrs trades are Market Makers getting their ducks in A row...but i could be wrong on that

comment.body:
Knew NVDA was looking bullish… but yesterday’s commentary was concerning. Now we are back to a bounce into OPEX. I feel whipsawed.

Wednesday night was : “They need liquidity first. How? Bounce. Mean reversion, bringing vol down”.

Yesterday morning was “if market rips, that’s spot up/vol up = low liquidity”. And,

“Therefore, my current tactic is a short vol trade, as I written premarket, without any directional take.”

Friday morning now it’s “What did I say? I said, I’m leaning towards a bounce into OPEX…. I called a bottom at 5532”

You can say “don’t get caught up in bull/bear”, but a bounce into OPEX and a bottom at 5532 is bullish. If that is the case, then taking a directional long (bullish) trade at close yesterday would’ve been a profitable move. Or at least building a position in shares/calls for next week expiry.

Are you still holding on to calls expiring next Friday Alma? If so, that context would’ve been nice to know yesterday, but I took you at your word that you weren’t positioned in any direction. If you disclosed this, then your “why not disperse positions into both ways?” comment today would stand on a lot firmer ground.

comment.body:
I do have sympathy for this view. I think what Alma does isidentify different paths, based on combinations of different conditions. Therefore she's not necessarily wrong to say "as expected", because when the conditions change, the path changes.
However most of the time she clearly has a preferred path, and I find it quite disingenuous for her or her followers to say everything is perfectly predicted even when the preferred path changes. Good example is when we were hovering above 6100. Alma’s lean was more up to come, but I was pointing out that repeated failure to sustain new highs looked like institutional distribution and was not bullish. I was told not to be so “one-dimensional” 😊. When the market rolled over, it was “as expected”.
The talent is clearly there. For me the key is clarity and transparency. No one is perfect and no one should be expected to be perfect.

comment.body:
If we bottomed here really, we want to see mkt above 5668 (level to conquer if vol crush happens, but it can change). However vol crushed has happened so probability for that event are rising. In addition weekly pivot is 3 point lower that level and this confirms the thesis even from momentum perspective.
Then we have JPM collar and here between 5565 and 6165 makes MM long Vanna so as spot move up they become longer Vega. And viceversa. This makes spot vol beta negative giving more power  to spot decay. Anyway if a bounce will occur and systematic flows will fuel it and vix exp is a good moment for that.
So I would say VOLATILITY to calm down and stay above 5653-5668

comment.body: With the preface that when I began reading Alma's material first on X and then after subscribing about 2 weeks ago, I felt like a true beginner (again).  And as someone who does not sell naked options, yesterday I thought about her post (vol down expected, supportive OpEx, her comment previously on bullish lean, that we needed a bump for more liquidity), and as importantly her educational posts, and with this in mind and using more typical trading strategy to find a setup/entry, I bought a call spread, the long leg close ATM and the short leg a further out OTM (PLTR 80/90). IV was of course juiced yesterday. The thinking was that the ATM leg has high gamma, if I get the directional move I'm expecting, then it goes ITM and as vol drops (as she signaled), the delta of that leg goes up, meanwhile the drop in vol has a suppressive effect on the OTM leg even with the move in spot.  And that's what happened.  Sold it this morning for solid profit.  I'd invite comment/input on any errors or flaws in this application of "selling vol", b/c as noted, I feel like I'm about at the level of my profile picture, a reasonably well trained dog.  But aspiring to reach child-like human proficiency soon :)

comment.body:
I'm not sure on how to estimate the probabilities of next week based on what you've said, but this is the gist of what I've got

We're still in a negative (synthetic or organic idk) MM GEX environment which still means based on that MM's are buying rallies and selling dips in general. From you post about the slope of speed increasing/steepening into march OPEX it would seem that effect would be exacerbated. We have supportive charm and Vanna tailwinds however as long as we stay above 5495.58 and that becomes more pronounced as time moves closer to expiration, so we can expect put decay from that and is reinforced above 5653.24. 

We're in an environment where we've seen IV underperform by A LOT (in almost the same amount of time and percentage down move in SPX in August IV was double-triple what it was at its peak the past few days). traders are shorting skew (skew is curve is flattening) and the past week we've really range bound, especially on NQ (almost a perfect rangebound box this entire week).

With JPM collar acting as a synthetic vanna flip, it makes sense it was easy to breach both to the downside and upside because of the decreasing vol. Not as important as after March 21st but there, what's more important is the systemic flows of puts closing out from selling in the vacuum that was created and the market finding a floor (either short term or longer term) and have a bit more liquidity to work with.

Modest buying from foreigners helps too but nothing crazy yet; short term stuff. What's interesting to me is that between the upside calls sold and 5565 puts on JPM collar, MM's are in a long vanna position, longer vega on pumps and dumping vega on dips. This is what generates the negative spot/vol corr and forces dealers to become longer delta as time passes to OpEx. 

This is all an attempt to bring in liquidity to bring the market down more so Powell is forced to QE and trump can have the 'glorious' economy he dreams of. It's an ego play for his legacy. JPM collar seems to be pretty important despite other mechanic flows because it's dictating the vanna and speed exposure for MM's. above the 5565 strike, it provides weak vanna support. the problem is the insane short gamma exposure MM's have with that, so it reverses the speed and vanna profiles.

From this, it's tough to say one way or another in terms of direction (shocker). But one this is clear is the short vol environment. That's why you continue to preach to look at vol cyclicality, and currently the vol regime has A TON of downward pressure on it (especially in relation to the charm and vanna flows). 

What I'm more so worried about this weekend and next Wednesday is FOMC. bad news is bad news and vice versa, and from other metrics I look at, I seem more downside into OpEx than anything else. 

How'd I do? lol

comment.body:
I get that things change daily, but she tends to conflate “intraday” posts with swing trading positions. If she talks about a JPM collar which will decay volatility as price moves down to 4700 towards expiration that is over 2 weeks away, and mentions a “breaking effect” can be felt around 5114.24, then that to me is consequential outside of just yesterday’s intraday price action. It clearly has implications on her March OPEX rally take, especially when she stated that price would oscillate in a more and more contracting channel. And that spot up would equate vol-up. 

And we closed far below the 5565 level yesterday, which should’ve been a concern considering this. And would actually support her thesis of shorting vol as it could be argued that we should go lower given the close and the environment we were in.

Instead today, we gap up, and rally hard, closing at highs of the day while volatility decays. And come to find, Alma was buying APPL and other calls below/at 5530 before close yesterday, along with long futures contracts.

You’d think she’d continue shorting volatility given the close. Which would mean she’d expect spot to come down further because price closed below the JPM strike at 5565.  And if her recommendation was to short vol yesterday, then why would she be buying calls? Because that would be contradictory to her “current tactic”, as price would have to continue to move down under 5565 in order to bring volatility down, according to her post yesterday.

She didn’t take any new positions today (at least this morning). She was riding ones she set up yesterday. So her analysis this morning was inconsequential to her current position for her March OPEX bounce.

So again, not very clear here. It certainly seemed obvious to me that we were due for a bounce. I even mentioned that was my expectation on Monday/Tuesday. But I was trying to stay honest towards Alma’s commentary yesterday, which obviously didn’t work out.

comment.body:
I think i understand where the confusion is. Ill try to break it down
1.) Volatility is down 8% but she said it would be up today.
VIX is a measure of 30-day ATM IV. OTM IV on 0DTE is a lot higher. For vol to go up on a longer basis we need to see an unwinding of puts and thats whats throwing everyone off. The short squeeze is getting stunted by 0DTE's (imo) and that isn't easy to see pre-market. Her posts are based on pre-market positioning.
2.) a 120 rip is 1.69% and it is higher, correct. But for you to make money your effectively betting that RV will exceed that. It could, but doing that on a 0DTE is fucking difficult because as the day goes on the prob ITM goes to 0. What if that rip comes at 4:20? Would you have held? etc etc
3.) This is more trading intraday vs longer term. She isn't shorting the Vix, she's probably shorting 1DTE PUTS. Ya it could've gone against her and she would've been blown out but the odds here aren't favoring downside moves.

lmk if this helps, i understand you're trying 
to learn. I do not trade 0DTE because i can't process volatility in realtime as fast lol

I understand your frustration, because I was confused by the same shit lmao. I would like to point out 2 things

Well you think wrong. Not everyone got it. Look back at the thread after she posted her intraday revision. Lots of people were unclear as what to expect or how to implement what she said.

But the post yesterday talked about vol down as price would move negatively towards the JPM 4700 strike. Any “rip” or “bounce” was described by her as “vol-up”, “spot-up”, especially in MAG8 names. She also said price was in an oscillating regime.

If she describes a ripping rally as being bad for vol decay, and describes that spot decay (lower SPX) would be good for Vol-decay as price moves towards a 4700, it seems the prospects of a large bounce into March OPEX would be far less likely. How would it not?

I don’t know how else to explain this.

Also, if there is vol decay on rips and we are in an oscillating range, then buying longer term calls in this environment for a March OPEX bounce would seem to be counter productive.

comment.body:
Spot up vol up in the context you’re using it in doesn’t seem to correlate to spot up VIX up, but spot up, IV of SPX options up (specifically calls). In negative MM GEX MM’s are buying the rally and selling the dump. 

If traders/customers are buying puts, who’s selling to them? If the MM’s/dealers are selling (shorting) the puts, they need to delta hedge by selling futures because that skews them to a long position, no? 

Not to mention the high negative gamma MM’s have from the JPM collar. Would that not start to play a pretty big roll the closer to expo that gets, adding to the negative gamma environment?


Volume today was pretty sparse, SPX volume was only this low on the way down since Feb 26th where we were also green and then continued down. If HF’s aren’t there like Alma said, that seems indicative to me of a still lack of liquidity. And a precarious place to be. 

Just thinking out loud, we’ll see what next week brings. Price action is doing exactly what I’ve wanted it to do this past week so the theory and thesis I have continues to be present. We shall see though as I have a peasants compared to what Alma is seeing and the info she can digest

Regarding Russia and Ukraine, I think it’s important to understand the real
reasons behind all this. The goal is to build up European defense and move Russia away from China. Both would then act as counterweights to the Chinese and smooth out the balance of power, since the U.S. doesn’t have the capacity anymore to enforce a global peace. This is similar to Disraeli’s foreign policy. The goal overall is radically reshape the global system while preventing China from becoming dominant and preventing the decline of the U.S.

comment.body:
VIX measures the IV of SPX options. So what is the difference? How could the IV of only calls be up (when IV affects calls and puts equally) and the VIX not be up? There also seems to be a conflation of trying to separate these two things, where one day VIX is a bad instrument that isn’t reflective of the IV of SPX options in this kind of context, then the next day Alma is using VIX in her Thursday analysis to state that volatility is sticky because it’s staying in the 26-27 range.

So which is it?

Please explain further you guys… and please explain what the VIX is if it doesn’t measure the IV of SPX options.

comment.body:
What I mean is, when IV rises it does so for both calls and puts. It’s not like it is a massive variance where the VIX is down by 8% and calls will still be higher.

To figure out the 1 day move for VIX you’d divide it by 16. So the decay is still the same.

comment.body:
IV 30days out I believe, not IV right now. I’m not entirely sure IV impacts calls and puts equally, in fact I lean towards the notion that it doesn’t, which is why it’s useful to look at. Skew tells you what’s being bid and what the market is trying to price in. That’s why it’s useful. If IV is rising faster for calls vs puts or vice versa over fixed strikes over x amount of expirations, what would that tell you?

I’ve started to monitor VIX3M and VVIX a lot more now after the spaces with volsignals and AJ (The Architect). I may or may not post charts every so often of IV Term structure and my own IV skews I’ll be looking at as well. It’s just one piece of the puzzle. 

I’ll say this too because I see part of where the frustration is coming from, Alma is not a God. She’s a human just like everyone else. You need to take what you can from here and run with it in other ways outside of just this Substack. I’m taking everything I’m learning here, and taking stuff from VolSignals, the Architect, Convex Value, etc. and applying what I’m learning here to the concepts they’re teaching as well (not sharing her charts outside of here, did that on X once and she rebuked me lol).

 Alma is great, I’m never not going to say that, but I KNOW she didn’t learn everything she knows from one single person, it’s been a multitude of people over the years. You shouldn’t do the same either. 

And don’t give up. Persistence is what separates winners from quitters.
